blocks.text: Chapter 7.
Habit Testing, and Where to Look

The last night of this story, friend. The machine is assembled, the morality weighed. What remains is the working question: you've built something, people are using it... how do you know if a habit is actually forming? Hope is not a metric. And gut feel, in this business, is how good products die guessing.

Eyal's answer is a discipline he calls Habit Testing — three steps, run in a loop, forever. And then a map: where in the world the next great hook is hiding, and why you have probably already touched it today without noticing.
The three-step loop

Step one: Identify. Dig into your data and find the users who already behave like devotees — the ones who return again and again without being begged. First, be honest about what "devoted" means for your product: a music app might expect daily visits, a shopping app weekly. Then count. If even five percent of your users hit that bar, you have a pulse — a habit cohort worth studying. If nobody does, no amount of growth spending will save you; you are pouring water into a cracked jar.

Step two: Codify. Now study your devotees like a naturalist studies a new species. What did they do differently? Not who they are — what path they walked. Maybe they all connected with three friends in the first week. Maybe they all saved a playlist on day one, or listed an item within a month. Somewhere in their behavior is a shared sequence — call it the Habit Path — the trail of actions that separates the hooked from the drifting. Your job is to find it and write it down.

Step three: Modify. Rebuild the product so it walks new users down that same trail — nudge them to connect the three friends, save the first playlist, take the step your devotees took. Then measure: did the habit cohort grow? And here is the word that makes it a discipline rather than a stunt: again. Identify, codify, modify — around and around, the product's own hook cycle, run by the builder on the product itself.
Where the next hook is hiding
But suppose you haven't built anything yet — where do you even look for a habit-shaped opportunity? Eyal offers a treasure map with three marks on it, and a flashlight for reading it.

The flashlight first: study your own behavior. The richest source of product insight is not a market report — it is the strange small things you already do. Scratch your own itch: the founder who notices their own workaround, their own compulsion, their own dead moment in the day, holds an insight no focus group can fake — and stands in the facilitator's square from birth.

Now the three marks on the map. Nascent behaviors — odd little things early adopters do that everyone else dismisses as toys. The giants of this era were all mocked in their cradles: short messages to nobody, air mattresses for strangers, videos of cats. When a fringe behavior is growing, not shrinking, pay attention — niche weirdness is how mainstream habits are born. Enabling technologies — every new wave of infrastructure opens a window where old behaviors become newly easy: faster networks made video effortless, phones made photos constant, each shift minting new habit fortunes. Ask: what just became dramatically easier? Something always did. And interface changes — whenever the way we touch machines changes, behaviors reshuffle: the mouse, the touchscreen, the voice assistant, each new surface a fresh deck of habit opportunities dealt to whoever is watching.
Pause and guess: One of the most instructive hooks Eyal studied isn't a social network or a game — it's a Bible app. Hundreds of millions of installs. What do you think made scripture habit-forming?
Ready? Here is what happened.

The full hook, humbly applied. The YouVersion Bible App sends its trigger at the moment of routine — a morning notification, timed to the reader's own rhythm. Action made effortless: reading plans that serve one short passage, and audio for the days you'd rather listen — the smallest possible unit of the behavior. Variable reward: daily verses arrive unpredictably chosen, fresh streams of commentary and community — the Rewards of the Self and Tribe, in vestments. Investment: streaks, bookmarks, highlights, years of notes — stored value that makes this Bible, of all Bibles, irreplaceable. The lesson cuts both ways and sums the whole book: the hook is genuinely content-agnostic — it can carry scripture as easily as slot machines. Which means the machinery will never decide what it serves. Only the builder ever does. And builders who claim their product is "different, too meaningful for this psychology stuff" — they are not above the hook; they are just running it badly.
The whole book in one breath
So here is Hooked, friend, the way I would whisper it if you only had an elevator ride:

Every habit begins with a trigger — outside you at first, inside you at last, and the inside ones run on small discomforts. The action follows when it's easy — ease beats desire, so count the steps and delete them. The reward must be uncertain — the maybe is the magnet — and it must scratch the true itch. Then the user invests, a little labor after the reward, and the labor becomes the lock while loading the next trigger. Run the loop until it hums on its own. Test it: find the devoted, learn their path, pave it for the rest. And before any of it — stand at the two-question gate: would I use this? Does it truly make lives better? Build only what passes.

And for the human holding the phone: you have the whole diagram now. The feeds that own you are not stronger than you — they are just running a loop you couldn't see. Now you see it. Name the trigger. Add the friction. Question the maybe. Count the stored value honestly. The loop only owns what it can hide.
The lantern by the door

That is the end of this story — but look around the library before you go. The Book Map will take you back through any chapter of this book in minutes, and the Distilled versions keep every detail sharp. And the shelf is longer than one book, friend: two shelves down, an ancient kingmaker is teaching a boy how power actually works. A shepherd is crossing a desert after a dream. A billionaire in a cheap T-shirt is waking two strangers before dawn. New fires are being lit all the time.
The seat by the storyteller stays warm for you. Whatever you open next — may your habits, from tonight, be the ones you chose.
Key takeaways
Habit Testing is a loop: Identify the devoted users, Codify their shared path, Modify the product to pave it — then repeat.
Even ~5% devoted users is a foundation; zero means fix the product, not the marketing.
Scratch your own itch: your own workarounds and compulsions are the richest product insight there is.
Watch three horizons: nascent behaviors (growing weirdness), enabling technologies (what just got easy?), interface changes (new surfaces, new habits).
The hook is content-agnostic — scripture or slot machines — so the builder's two-question gate is the only conscience it will ever have.
The loop only owns what it can hide: name the trigger, add friction, question the maybe, count your stored value.
